headline: Budget cut fallout: After Idaho cut a critical Medicaid mental health service, two patients died
summary: Idaho faces a budget shortfall that prompted Gov. Brad Little to order state agency spending cuts, while the Legislature considers further reductions to fund tax cuts, including a proposed $45 million cut to public assistance programs next fiscal year. The governor's proposal would slash doctor pay rates by half and eliminate services like disability support or Medicaid dental coverage for the remainder. The Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee approved additional 1-2% agency cuts atop the governor's 3% reductions, though Medicaid and Health & Welfare were exempted from these latest reductions. State health officials describe current cuts as having gone "through muscle" and being "down to bone," with the eliminated Assertive Community Treatment mental health program costing $1.3 million to restore for the rest of this fiscal year and approximately $4 million annually thereafter.

headline: Additional budget cuts may cause Idaho to ‘go backwards’ on medical education progress
summary: The Idaho Legislature's Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee approved additional budget cuts of 1% ($131M) for FY 2026 and 2% ($143M) for FY 2027 to comply with federal tax cuts, which would halt a planned Pocatello obstetrics fellowship and jeopardize existing medical residency obligations under the state's 10-year GME Strategic Plan. The reductions come on top of prior 3% cuts, with the state currently funding only about $60,000 of the $250,000 annual cost per resident. The budget cuts require full legislative approval before enactment.

headline: For Indigenous Americans it’s unthinkable, but true. ICE is arresting, detaining Native Americans.
summary: ICE officers are detaining Native Americans across the U.S., including members of tribes in Idaho, erroneously identifying them as immigrants and raising concerns about treaty violations and citizenship rights. In response, tribal governments like the Shoshone-Bannock Tribes in Idaho are advising citizens to carry tribal identification cards and photos of them on their phones. Tribes are also asserting sovereignty by banning ICE from tribal property and requiring judicial warrants for entry, as seen with the Lac du Flambeau Band of Lake Superior Chippewa Indians and the Ho-Chunk Nation. The Oklahoma Inter-Tribal Council of the Five Civilized Tribes has approved a resolution demanding government-to-government cooperation with ICE officials. These actions highlight a conflict between federal immigration enforcement and tribal sovereignty, with Indigenous citizens feeling targeted and "less than full citizens."

headline: The kids are not alright — ICE threatens children’s short-term health, long-term prospects
summary: A Trump executive order rescinded the sensitive areas policy protecting Minnesota schools from immigration enforcement, prompting a lawsuit from Duluth and Fridley Public Schools and Education Minnesota alleging Administrative Procedures Act violations. The deployment of approximately 2,300 federal agents has created a climate of fear, leading to mass student absenteeism, with some classes dropping from 20 to 4-5 in-person students. This absenteeism triggers a state law that drops students from enrollment after 15 consecutive missed days, putting district funding at risk. Federal agents have detained parents at bus stops, boarded school vans with students present, and used school parking lots as staging areas. The enforcement actions, affecting citizens and legal immigrants, have resulted in community support networks, documented mental health crises, and academic disruptions across the Twin Cities.

headline: A morning at the Wyoming Capitol: How the budget session’s first day unfolded
summary: The Wyoming Legislature's budget session opened with major fiscal policy conflicts: Governor Gordon opposed legislative proposals to defund the Wyoming Business Council and instead recommended a reform task force; he urged lawmakers to reconsider opting into the SUN Bucks summer food assistance program after last year's opt-out; legislators rejected Gordon's across-the-board state employee pay raises, approving increases only for specific positions like plow drivers and Highway Patrol; and significant cuts were proposed to institutions including the University of Wyoming, setting up contentious budget debates.

headline: State of the State: Gordon urges lawmakers to restore his $11.1B budget
summary: Governor Mark Gordon urged lawmakers to restore his $11.1 billion biennial budget after the Joint Appropriations Committee (JAC) finalized significant alterations, including dismantling the Wyoming Business Council, cutting $40 million from the University of Wyoming, and denying $111.8 million in state employee pay adjustments. The JAC's changes were driven by Wyoming Freedom Caucus members pursuing an "anti-acceleration" spending approach. Gordon advocated for moving a $250 million revenue windfall into permanent savings rather than using it to offset cuts, while proposing a task force to reform the Business Council instead of defunding it. He emphasized the economic and human costs of austerity, highlighting impacts on business development, higher education competitiveness, and state workforce retention. The budget session will determine which of the governor's proposals or the JAC's cuts will prevail through mid-March.

headline: Wyoming Legislature live: The 68th Legislature begins its budget session
summary: The Wyoming Legislature's budget session opened with the Joint Appropriations Committee recommending major cuts to the University of Wyoming and defunding the Wyoming Business Council, while lawmakers anticipate a contentious battle over education funding under supermajority voting requirements for bills.

headline: School funding bill dies in Wyoming House
summary: The Wyoming House failed to introduce House Bill 110 for K-12 public school finance recalibration by a single vote (41-21, needing 42), jeopardizing the state's constitutional funding obligation and compliance with a 2025 court order mandating adequate school funding. The bill's teacher pay raises were overshadowed by bipartisan opposition to provisions increasing class sizes and mandating state health insurance participation, though an identical Senate version (SF 81) remains viable for the budget session.

headline: Missouri lawmakers scour for savings to shield disability, health programs from cuts
summary: Missouri faces a "budget calamity" with declining revenues and shrinking surplus, prompting lawmakers to identify cuts for FY2027. Governor Kehoe proposes $17.5 million in cuts to disability self-directed supports and $61.7 million from day habilitation programs, plus over $2.7 million in reductions to substance use and tobacco prevention grants. Bipartisan lawmakers, including Republican budget subcommittee chair Darin Chappell and Democratic Rep. Raychel Proudie, vow to resist cuts to vital disability and public health services. The Department of Health and Senior Services, which spends only $7 per person on public health, reported $52-65 million in unspent federal funds annually and proposes reducing its budget authority by $10 million to "right size" spending. Despite these efforts, officials acknowledge painful cuts to essential programs remain unavoidable.

headline: National Fight Over Immigration Enforcement Roils Maui County
summary: A county's agreement with the FBI has alarmed activists and council members over potential immigration enforcement implications, possibly involving local law enforcement cooperation with federal authorities, information sharing, or detention facility usage, prompting political debate about the county's role in federal immigration policy and calls for transparency regarding impacts on immigrant communities.

headline: From Kent State to Minneapolis
summary: The article connects the 1970 Kent State shootings—where Ohio National Guard killed four student protesters—to recent civilian deaths in Minneapolis, framing both as state violence against First Amendment rights. It argues that government use of lethal force against protesters fractures the social contract and erodes constitutional protections for assembly and petition, warning that when protest is treated as a threat rather than a right, democratic freedoms become performance and signal the quiet erosion of constitutional democracy.

headline: CT group calls for freedom for Dr. Abu Safiya
summary: A Connecticut-organized "Campaign to Free Dr. Abu Safiya" has mobilized 100 signers—including several state legislators and Nobel laureate Jody Williams—for an open letter protesting Israel's 13-month detention of the pediatrician and other medical workers without evidence; the campaign has held public actions such as a "Poster Shout Out" at an award ceremony and a Zoom reading of Dr. Abu Safiya's final posts, challenging his designation as an "Unlawful Combatant" and demanding immediate release.

headline: Nebraskans raise alarm about proposed $152 million cut to DHHS
summary: Governor Pillen's budget proposes cutting $18 million from Nebraska's retroactive Medicaid eligibility, reducing coverage from 90 to 30 days; over 20 healthcare professionals and groups, including Children's Nebraska and the Nebraska Hospital Association, warned the Appropriations Committee this would raise costs, limit access for vulnerable patients—especially sick infants—and create a "hidden tax" on providers, while DHHS officials argued the cuts are manageable and most applications are already processed within 10 days.

headline: Community members fight for Froid man facing immigration charge
summary: About two dozen Froid community members rallied at the Missouri River Courthouse in Great Falls to support Roberto Orozco-Ramirez at his federal immigration hearing, traveling nearly seven hours to protest his detention. The demonstration reflects mounting public frustration with Trump Administration deportation efforts and federal immigration enforcement tactics statewide. Community members challenged the government's portrayal of Orozco-Ramirez as a criminal despite his clean record and essential local business. The rally underscores broader tensions as Montana municipalities grapple with federal immigration agent operations in their towns. Supporters are channeling their response into political mobilization, urging calls to local and state politicians for an Order of Supervision to allow Orozco-Ramirez to remain.

headline: Senate Republicans unveil income tax cut proposal tied to broad cuts in ‘corporate welfare’
summary: Georgia Senate Republicans proposed tax legislation that would reduce state revenues through income tax cuts to 4.99% while saving hundreds of millions by eliminating corporate tax credits, with a separate House plan costing over $750 million in future revenues and a $1.2 billion rebate proposal, as lawmakers advance a long-term goal to eliminate the state income tax by 2032.

headline: NM Transportation secretary to resign
summary: Warnings about dire state road conditions preceding the Transportation Secretary's resignation highlight infrastructure governance failures that could become a catalyst for public protests or civil unrest.

headline: New Mexico Senate committee weighs economic toll of new law as counties fear ICE detention closures
summary: New Mexico's Immigrant Safety Act (HB9), which prohibits state government bodies from entering ICE detention contracts, has triggered urgent Senate Finance Committee hearings to assess economic impacts in Cibola, Torrance, and Otero counties before the legislative session ends Feb. 19. The law could cause detention center closures, eliminating 284 jobs ($21 million in salaries) in Otero County alone, while counties with privately-owned facilities face uncertainty about CoreCivic's contract renewal plans. Lawmakers are debating how to support communities potentially losing a major employer without "throwing money at an issue," highlighting tensions between immigrant protection policies and rural economic dependence on detention facilities.

headline: Excess school taxes and minimum state funding: Lawmakers move education funding bills forward
summary: The South Dakota Legislature is advancing two K-12 funding bills: Senate Bill 223 would lower petition signature thresholds and extend timelines for voters to challenge school property tax opt-outs, while a House bill would change state law to guarantee education funding increases of at least 3% or inflation (whichever is greater), aiming to protect teacher salaries and prevent cost shifts to local taxpayers.

headline: Advocates, protesters rally against ‘conversion therapy’ bill in Iowa House
summary: Protesters gathered at the Iowa State Capitol on Feb. 9, 2026, chanting "shame" at lawmakers leaving a subcommittee meeting that approved House Study Bill 669, which opponents say would legalize conversion therapy for transgender children. Demonstrators also shouted "love not hate" and "trans rights are human rights" throughout the meeting. The bill would exempt parents' responses to children's gender dysphoria from child abuse definitions, allowing practices like refusing gender-affirming care or names/pronouns. Rep. Steve Holt criticized the protests as a "heckler's veto" attempting to drown out opposing views, while stating he would advance the legislation. The measure, supported by Christian conservative groups, will next be considered by the full House Judiciary Committee.

headline: Benson says as governor, there is ‘a moral responsibility’ to address Black maternal health care
summary: Michigan gubernatorial candidate Jocelyn Benson unveiled a health care platform targeting the Black maternal health crisis, highlighting that Black women face triple the pregnancy-related mortality risk of white women, with policy proposals including an independent prescription drug affordability board and hospital cost transparency requirements to address disparities in rural and urban communities like Flint.

headline: Detroit-area elected officials call for release of Venezuelan asylum seekers detained in Hazel Park
summary: ICE detained two Michigan asylum seekers with work authorization near a Hazel Park Amazon facility on Feb. 2; both previously held Temporary Protected Status suspended under the Trump administration in 2025, a policy under legal challenge. State legislators Stephanie Chang, Mary Cavanagh, Rep. Veronica Paiz, and Detroit Council Member Gabriela Santiago-Romero demanded their release from the North Lake Processing Center, Michigan's largest ICE detention facility operated by GEO Group. The men's partners emphasize their role as family providers and their non-criminal status. The TPS suspension's legality may support federal habeas corpus petitions. The case exemplifies conflict between federal enforcement and state/local immigrant protection efforts.

headline: House passes ‘Make West Virginia Healthy’ bill
summary: House Bill 4982, the Make West Virginia Healthy Act of 2026, passed the House 91-4 to address West Virginia's leading chronic disease rates by empowering the Office of Healthy Lifestyles to coordinate with the Department of Education on student nutrition and physical fitness programs, establishing a Medicaid "Food is Medicine" initiative, and creating county grant programs; the bill mandates reestablishing the Presidential Fitness Test to secure federal Rural Health Transformation Program funding despite concerns about student trauma, while requiring anonymized student health metrics for program accountability.

headline: Senate says WV law enforcement must notify ICE if they’ve identified an undocumented immigrant
summary: West Virginia's Senate passed Senate Bill 615 (32-2 vote) requiring law enforcement to notify ICE about undocumented immigrants, with Republicans supporting federal immigration cooperation amid President Trump's crackdown while Democrats opposed citing recent federal court rulings that found ICE violated due process rights and ordered immigrant releases; the state participates in ICE's 287(g) program and receives $90 per day for each detainee held in its jails.

headline: NC Republican legislators grill Mecklenburg sheriff over jail operation and ICE cooperation
summary: State legislators questioned Mecklenburg County Sheriff Garry McFadden about his cooperation with ICE during a hearing triggered by the stabbing death of Ukrainian refugee Iryna Zarutska, highlighting partisan divisions over local-federal immigration enforcement. Republican lawmakers criticized the Democratic sheriff's policies while McFadden claimed he was targeted for being progressive and Black. Committee Chairman Brenden Jones blamed local Democratic officials for enabling crime, contrasting with praise for Charlotte Mayor Vi Lyles. The hearing also addressed 21 jail deaths under McFadden's tenure and the sheriff's claim of selective scrutiny. Democrats countered that the focus on Charlotte crime distracts from the legislature's failure to pass a state budget.

headline: Congresswoman Deborah Ross on efforts to place guardrails on Homeland Security and ICE
summary: Congresswoman Deborah Ross (NC-02) has joined a bipartisan chorus of elected leaders pushing back against the Trump administration's immigration enforcement policies, which she described as "destructive and frequently lawless attacks on immigrant communities," according to a recent NC Newsline interview.

headline: Dr. Shannon Schumacher of KFF on the latest national polling results on health care costs
summary: New KFF polling analyzed by senior survey analyst Dr. Shannon Schumacher reveals that large majorities of Americans are deeply concerned about the nation's affordability crisis, particularly in health care, with millions reporting they are worse off than a year ago due to soaring costs and uncertain insurance coverage. The poll findings show widespread dissatisfaction with national political leaders and concern about policies, actions, and inactions of the Trump administration and congressional leaders. These health care cost concerns are emerging as a significant policy issue as the 2026 elections approach.

headline: This Ballad hospital, flooded by Hurricane Helene, will be rebuilt for $44M in a flood plain
summary: Ballad Health is rebuilding the flood-destroyed Unicoi County Hospital on a new site that climate data firms identify as having significant flood risk, potentially exceeding the old location's vulnerability; despite receiving $7.4 million in FEMA funding, the hospital system is relying on outdated federal flood maps that don't account for climate change, while experts recommend elevating the facility 8-18 feet to withstand 1,000-year floods, and the land purchase involves a state legislator whose family owns the property, highlighting governance concerns about infrastructure planning for essential healthcare services in a state-sanctioned monopoly system.

headline: Un solicitante de asilo prosperaba en Oklahoma hasta una detención de ICE
summary: Colombian asylum seeker Alejandro Toro Sepúlveda self-deported with his family (including two U.S.-citizen children) after ICE detained them in Texas, despite his pending asylum case, valid work authorization, and no criminal record. His Oklahoma employer Krysta Henry condemned the enforcement as contradicting Trump administration claims of targeting criminals. State Senator Michael Brooks Jiménez confirmed increased ICE patrols in Latino neighborhoods and arrests of asylum seekers at routine check-ins, highlighting a policy shift toward detaining non-criminal immigrants.

headline: Oklahoma Senate leader criticizes proposed property tax elimination on homesteads
summary: A proposed initiative (State Question 843) to eliminate property taxes for homeowners would reduce state and local revenue for schools, counties, and public safety, with Senate President Pro Tem Paxton warning of shifted burdens onto businesses and farmers while supporters argue the $92,263-signature petition would force lawmakers to offset losses by cutting corporate tax credits and incentives. The proposal has prompted legislative alternatives including SJR 39 to cap property tax increases, SB 1809 to raise homestead exemptions to $5,000, and SJR 23 to exempt seniors over 65 while allowing counties to levy consumption taxes. Former Rep. Reynolds claims the measure stops "giving away taxpayer dollars to corporations," while Paxton calls it "immature" and insists reform was already underway. The dispute directly impacts Oklahoma's budget structure by challenging property tax revenue essential for public services.

headline: Trump versus Massie tensions flare at local Kentucky event, on national stage
summary: Kentucky's escalating Republican primary conflict, highlighted by state House Speaker David Osborne seizing a microphone from Rep. Thomas Massie at a Lincoln Day Dinner after Massie defended his wife from Trump's attacks, may generate political protests or rallies as the May 19 election nears, with deep divisions over Trump's endorsements and inflammatory campaign rhetoric creating potential for civil unrest among competing party factions.

headline: Embattled cultural resources commissioner resigns amid Merrimack ICE facility fallout
summary: New Hampshire Natural and Cultural Resources Commissioner Sarah Stewart resigned after revelations that her department knew about ICE plans for a detention facility in Merrimack but failed to inform Gov. Kelly Ayotte. Emails obtained by the ACLU showed departmental staff communicated with ICE in early January, contradicting Ayotte's statements that she received no federal confirmation. Councilors chastised Stewart for a leadership failure that allowed critical immigration enforcement information to be withheld from the governor's office. The facility is part of a reported Trump administration network of detention centers to support expanded deportation efforts. Ayotte will nominate an acting commissioner while searching for a permanent replacement.

headline: California gasta millones en base a datos de este rastreador de polución. ¿Quién se beneficia de su última actualización?
summary: California officials are holding public meetings to gather community opinions on the updated CalEnviroScreen tool that allocates environmental cleanup funds, while activists criticize data gaps that exclude some contaminated communities and argue the state fails to translate public input into aggressive permit denial for polluting facilities, highlighting a gap between opinion collection and policy implementation.

headline: Bill would require Kansas schools to stock naloxone, provide fentanyl prevention education
summary: House Bill 2489 would mandate Kansas schools maintain naloxone supplies and provide fentanyl prevention education for grades 9-12 to address rising youth overdose deaths, with 48 children dying from fentanyl between 2020-2023. The legislation allows voluntary administration by non-medical school personnel, raising concerns from the Kansas National Education Association about expectations for untrained staff. The State Board of Education would develop age-appropriate curriculum guidance. A Kansas City firefighter testified that every school where he presented overdose prevention education subsequently experienced an on-campus overdose. The bill frames naloxone access as equivalent to fire extinguishers or defibrillators—critical emergency tools for public health crises.

headline: Alaska legislators say governor’s fiscal plan is likely dead after first week of hearings
summary: Alaska faces a structural budget deficit as oil and investment revenues fail to keep pace with service demands and Permanent Fund dividends, leading to 16.6% inflation-adjusted cuts to state agencies and 80% cuts to capital construction since 2015. The resulting fiscal crisis has left public schools decaying and facing a constitutional lawsuit, while the state has forgone an estimated 2-3% of GDP growth over the past decade due to the lack of a long-term fiscal plan. Governor Dunleavy's comprehensive proposal, centered on a seasonal statewide sales tax and Permanent Fund restructuring, appears dead on arrival after unanimous public opposition and near-zero legislative support. The plan's rejection highlights the political impossibility of new taxes, even as lawmakers have cut the statutory Permanent Fund dividend every year since 2016 and face continued pressure to maintain services. Without action, the state remains dependent on volatile oil revenues and the uncertain prospect of a natural gas pipeline to achieve future fiscal stability.
